name: ESP32 DevKit V1
tagline: Dual-core 240MHz MCU with built-in WiFi + Bluetooth — the powerhouse of IoT projects
description: The ESP32 is a feature-rich microcontroller with a dual-core 240MHz Xtensa LX6 CPU, 520KB SRAM, 4MB flash, integrated 802.11 b/g/n WiFi, and Bluetooth 4.2/BLE. It has 34 programmable GPIO pins, hardware I2C/SPI/UART, touch sensors, hall sensor, and a DAC. Used in advanced projects requiring wireless communication, real-time OS (FreeRTOS), or dual-core task separation.

specs.operatingVoltage: 3.3V logic (5V tolerant via USB, onboard LDO)

pinout.name: Enable
pinout.desc: Active HIGH chip enable. Pull LOW to reset.

pinout.name: Input Only
pinout.desc: Input-only ADC pins — no output, no pull-up

pinout.name: TX0/RX0
pinout.desc: Hardware UART0 — also used for USB flashing (avoid in production code)

pinout.name: Boot
pinout.desc: Must be HIGH at boot. Pull LOW to enter download mode.

pinout.name: Onboard LED
pinout.desc: Connected to onboard blue LED on most DevKit boards

pinout.name: 5V In
pinout.desc: 5V input from external supply via onboard LDO

wiringNote: GPIO6–11 are connected to internal flash — NEVER use these as GPIO, it will crash the chip. Avoid GPIO34/35/36/39 for output. Always use GPIO.begin() or Wire.begin(SDA_PIN, SCL_PIN) to assign I2C pins explicitly on ESP32.
libraryInstall: Arduino IDE → File → Preferences → Additional Boards URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/espressif/arduino-esp32/gh-pages/package_esp32_index.json → Boards Manager → Install ESP32
commonMistakes: Using GPIO6–11 — these are internal flash pins, using them crashes the chip
Not specifying SDA/SCL in Wire.begin() — defaults may not match your wiring
GPIO0 floating at boot → enters download mode randomly
Forgetting that ADC2 pins are disabled when WiFi is active
Using delay() in FreeRTOS tasks — use vTaskDelay() instead
